scenarios.label: Classical RSA (Legacy)
scenarios.description: Traditional TLS 1.3 with RSA certificate and X25519 key exchange. Represents current widespread deployment — quantum-vulnerable.
considerations: RSA private keys must be replaced before Q-day
Harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks are already occurring
Certificate re-issuance required — plan PKI inventory now
NIST recommends deprecating RSA-2048 by 2030

scenarios.label: Classical ECDSA P-256
scenarios.description: Modern TLS 1.3 with ECDSA certificate and X25519 key exchange. Current best practice for classical deployments.
considerations: Elliptic curve private keys are vulnerable to Shor's algorithm
Better performance than RSA but same quantum risk
Widely deployed; migration requires new certificate issuance
Begin crypto-agility planning to support parallel PQC certificates

scenarios.label: Hybrid PQC (Recommended Migration Path)
scenarios.description: Hybrid X25519+ML-KEM-768 key exchange with classical ECDSA certificate. Recommended by IETF and NIST for near-term migration — protects key exchange against quantum adversaries today.
considerations: Protects session keys against HNDL (harvest-now-decrypt-later) attacks
Authentication still relies on classical ECDSA — upgrade certificates next
Chrome, Firefox, and Cloudflare have deployed this configuration
~2 KB additional handshake overhead — minimal performance impact
Compliant with CISA/NIST hybrid guidance for transition period

scenarios.label: Full Hybrid PQC (Key Exchange + Auth)
scenarios.description: Hybrid key exchange and PQC certificate (ML-DSA-44). Protects both session establishment and identity authentication against quantum adversaries.
considerations: Fully quantum-safe handshake when combined with trusted PQC CA hierarchy
ML-DSA-44 signature: ~2.4 KB vs ECDSA P-256: ~71 bytes
Total handshake ~5-8 KB larger than classical — still acceptable for most use cases
Requires PQC-capable CA infrastructure and certificate issuance pipeline
Draft RFC support: draft-ietf-tls-mldsa, FIPS 204 (final)

scenarios.label: Pure PQC — ML-DSA-65 + ML-KEM-1024
scenarios.description: Maximum quantum assurance. ML-DSA-65 authentication (Security Level 3) and ML-KEM-1024 key encapsulation (Security Level 5). For sensitive data with very long confidentiality requirements.
considerations: Highest post-quantum security assurance
Handshake ~10-12 KB — check MTU and connection latency budget
Pure PQC (no classical fallback) — ensure all endpoints support FIPS 203/204
Not yet widely deployed in production; suitable for high-security pilot deployments
Use in CNSA 2.0 compliant systems per NSA guidance

migration_phases.label: Inventory
migration_phases.description: Identify all TLS endpoints, certificates, and cipher suites in use
actions: Run CBOM scan to enumerate all TLS libraries and versions
Catalogue certificate algorithms (RSA vs ECDSA), key sizes, and expiry dates
Identify connections carrying long-lived sensitive data (priority for HNDL mitigation)

migration_phases.label: Hybrid Key Exchange
migration_phases.description: Deploy X25519+ML-KEM-768 for key exchange on priority endpoints
actions: Update TLS libraries to versions supporting ML-KEM hybrid groups
Enable X25519MLKEM768 as preferred key exchange group
Monitor handshake size and latency impact

migration_phases.label: PQC Certificates
migration_phases.description: Issue ML-DSA certificates from PQC-capable CA
actions: Deploy PQC intermediate CA (ML-DSA-65 or ML-DSA-87)
Issue ML-DSA end-entity certificates for high-priority services
Configure dual-certificate serving for backward compatibility
Update OCSP and CRL infrastructure to support larger PQC signatures

migration_phases.label: Full PQC
migration_phases.description: Retire classical algorithms once PQC coverage is complete
actions: Disable legacy RSA and ECDSA cipher suites
Ensure all CA trust anchors are PQC-capable
Update monitoring and alerting for PQC certificate validity
Document FIPS 203/204 compliance in security attestations
